turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Siegal, you may proceed.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. The petitioner, Vincent Joseph Spano, was convicted of a crime of murder in the first degree in the County Court, Bronx County, New York, was sentenced to death. The sentence has been stayed. Certiorari was granted by this Honorable Court on the 15th day of December, 1958. The Court of Appeals of the State of New York affirmed the judgment of conviction by a vote of 4-to-3. Now, may it please the Court, briefly the facts are these. On January 22nd, 1957, the petitioner, Vincent Joseph Spano, was in a bar and grill and had a $10-bill on the counter when the deceased, one Frank Palermo walked in and took the bill off the counter and walked out of the bar. Vincent Joseph Spano walked out after him, an argument started and Frank Palermo, a former professional fighter gave the petitioner Spano a most vicious beating, punching him down to the ground and kicking him in the head, leaving him in a dazed and bloody condition. He became violently sick immediately, vomited right in front of him, was taken into the bar. Someone there washed his bruises. He went home, walked home, came back and went to the candy store that he was directed to go to by Palermo. When Palermo gave Spano this beating he said, “This isn't all. Bring the rest of the money to this candy store,” designating a certain candy store.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What did he mean by (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, he had made a demand for certain money and all he had gotten, sir, was $10 off the bar.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, you mean (Inaudible) man claiming that he owed it or --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Not that he owed it. We contend, sir, that it was a matter of tribute, was a matter of extortion. Palermo directed Spano to go to this designated candy store. That to anyone in that neighborhood, amongst those people, was known as the headquarters of Palermo and when Spano got in there, as the evidence shows, three gangsters were in the place besides Palermo. Spano had gone home in his dazed condition, had gotten a gun, had walked to the candy store and at first fired a volley of two shots up near the top of the ceiling, no where's near or in the direction of Palermo. The evidence shows that at that time, as the petitioner stated the door behind him closed or opened and he felt someone running towards him from behind, the three in the store closing in on him and it was at that time that he fired a volley of three shots and killed Palermo. He then, as the evidence showed, left the scene and was missing for a period of about I believe it was 10 days or two weeks, something of that order. It then developed that Spano got in touch with an attorney named Tobias Russo, a well-known lawyer in Bronx and arranged for Mr. Russo to surrender to the authorities. In the meantime before the surrender, the District Attorney of Bronx County had presented the evidence that they had before the Grand Jury of Bronx County and obtained an indictment charging this defendant with the crime of murder in the first degree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where there eyewitnesses to the murder?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: There was one other. There was one eyewitness, sir, a young boy I think 14 or 15 years of age. The attorney, Mr. Russo made arrangements with the District Attorney, met the District Attorney in the street in front of the County Court House at or about 7:30 p.m. and there turned over, the petitioner Spano to the District Attorney, Personal Assistant District Attorney, Goldsmith and the county detective and then left. The testimony shows that the Assistant District Attorney and the police talked the petitioner to a District Attorney's office. This was about 7:30 or quarter to 8 p.m.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Did the District Attorney have an arrest warrant at that time?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Did the District Attorney have an arrest warrant?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, it was a bench warrant notwithstanding, when the indictment was filed by the Grand Jury and accepted by the Court a bench warrant was issued.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Was the arrest made under that?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: I would -- I would say. He was surrendered pursuant to the bench warrant and indictment. The Assistant District Attorney interrogate or attempted to interrogate Spano from 7:30 until 12, or 12 o'clock in the morning and during all that time, Spano said, “I want my attorney.” The District Attorney knew he had an attorney because only a few moments ago he had been surrendered by his attorney upon arrangement. The District Attorney, nevertheless, knowing that he had an attorney and being told by Spano, “I have been advised not to make any statement except to give you my name. I want to consult with my attorney.” Nevertheless, they continued to interrogate and interrogate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Had the District Attorney indicated to Spano's attorney that he intended to interrogate him?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: No, sir, not a word, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Had the -- Spano's attorney indicated to the District Attorney that he didn't expect him to be interrogated, had he given many instructions?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Not to the District Attorney.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not to District Attorney.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Only to his client.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To his client.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All this --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- all this questioning took place after the indictment, is that correct?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: After the indictment, yes, sir.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: What was the provision of the bench warrant? What did it require (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, the bench warrant, reads as follows, sir. “You are therefore commanded forthwith to arrest the above name and bring him before that Court to answer the indictment or if the courts have adjourned for the term that you deliver him into the custody of a sheriff of the county of or in the City of New York to the warden of the city prison of the City of New York.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, now, was that -- was that command complied with?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: They didn't take him and put him into the city prison. They took him into the District Attorney's Office, sir, and questioned him until 12:30 and when they saw at 12:30 in the morning that he's still persistent, that he --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Were the courts open or was this term?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, the Court was open, sir, but may I say this, sir. The District Attorney is present as a custom in the Bronx that when a man is arrested on a warrant after indictment, no matter what time, he is brought before a county judge even if it's at his home because they all live right there in the neighborhood. There wasn't any -- any trouble at all about getting a judge if they wanted him at that hour.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, I gather the Court there it was termed time. The courts were actually sitting during the day.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: That is right, the time was not called. They were actually sitting. They were going to sit the next morning at 10 o'clock. Now, sir, after, 12:30, they saw they couldn't get anywhere, they took the petitioner up to the squad room of a police station and there continued to interrogate him and he there again, asked for his attorney, time and time again. At time, one of the police officers testified that he couldn't find the -- a lawyer with the said name or telephone number in the telephone book. At any rate, he never got an attorney. At any rate, during that time, the District Attorney and the police sent a police officer named Bruno, who was a friend of Spano and to Spano a number of times, to tell him that he, Bruno, was in trouble if he didn't make -- if Spano didn't make a statement in this case, that his wife was going to have another child. He might lose his job. And he insisted that he make a statement. The reason for that is this. While the defendant or the petitioner was out before he arranged for the surrender, he called his friend Bruno and told him what had happened and said that Palermo had been on his back and had given him a bad beating and that he was dazed and so forth that he killed him and he wanted advice, that he was going to get a lawyer. So now, they use this same friend, Bruno, to coerce Spano into making a statement. They used him for the purpose of having him disregard, if they could, the advice and instructions of his attorney. The District Attorney all of the time knew that this man was represented by an attorney, knew that there had been an indictment, knew that there was a judicial proceeding pending, yet nevertheless, continued to interrogate and interrogate until sometime around 3:30 a.m., after Bruno had left Spano for a few minutes, Spano said, “All right, let me make a statement.” They called in a stenographer and they got a statement.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What were the lengths of time of continuous questioning undisputedly not -- not --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, what we --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- had given answer to which the district -- which the -- which the District Attorney will have -- the District Attorney will have to agree. That's what I mean by (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: My best opinion, sir, that the undisputed testimony is that it occurred between 7:30 and 12:30, then -- that might have taken a half hour to get to the squad room and then from about 1 to 3:30 the next morning.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Anyhow?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: That's in the District Attorney's Office. Then they took him up to the squad room and they went to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) during those -- those four hours (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, they say they gave him a cup of coffee or they gave him a sandwich or something during that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The questioning --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: -- time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- continuous?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How many people were in the room at the time?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, testimony was that they went in and out three ways. Those three police officers. The Assistant District Attorney was there all the time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Who did the questioning?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Mr. Goldsmith, the Assistant District Attorney and the police officers.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was the stenographer present or just (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: The stenographer was only present, sir, at about 3:30 when they asked him to come in. There was evidence of a case of a beating but I am not arguing that point now because that was denied and it's not -- it's a disputed of fact. It's not an undisputed fact.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How long was the interruption after (Inaudible) which is --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: I would say in all about --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: -- half hour.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Then it lasted until 3 o'clock?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: 3 o'clock, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: 3 o'clock. And then was the stenographer called in?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He was there?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How long was that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: The exact time, sir, is 3:20 a.m. to 3:30 a.m.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: The indictment has been returned a week or so maybe --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Prior, yes, sir. There was a judicial proceeding pending at the time, when the bench warrant was issued. He was surrendered pursuant to the indictment of the bench warrant.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: In your State that the indictment specifies the degree of the murder charge does it?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Is he indicted of the first degree murder?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: That's right, sir. He was indicted for first degree murder.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: First degree murder.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Which as a matter of fact for meditation and deliberation.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And that do I understand he's been convicted as to a first degree murder?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: He has been convicted of first degree murder and sentenced to death.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any indication of physical abuse?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: There was, sir, throughout the record but it is not an undisputed fact. They -- the police officers denied it and for that reason I refrain from arguing it here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the admissibility of his confession go to the jury?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, over objection, the admissibility went to the jury but not on the question of the absence of counsel or the demand for counsel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what I was wondering was whether your position here turns solely on the absence of the counsel or whether you make a broader contention that absence of counsel taken in to consideration that the whole -- the whole setting should have excluded his confession --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Absence --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- as a matter of due process?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Absence of counsel, sir, plus the fact that there had been an indictment, plus the fact that there was a bench warrant issue, plus the fact that there was a judicial proceeding in being plus all of the other circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you claim this is a coerced confession?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: In -- in that light, yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't understand what you mean in that light.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, it's a coerced confession. May I say this. It's a coerced confession in the -- in the sense or in the language of Judge Desmond of the Court of Appeals in the dissenting opinion. And may I answer this way, sir? Well, Judge Desmond said this, “Usually, of course, the test for admissibility of a confession is voluntariness and usually voluntariness is a question of fact for the jury.” But one of the senses in which the courts used voluntary, a confession or incriminating testimony taken from a defendant without warranting of his rights, during the progress of a criminal cause against them is not voluntary since in such a case the fact is undisputed. The question is not one for the jury but a constitutional question of due process, an undisputed fact. Some violations of rights, are too fundamental to be excused by a jury or layman, directly and frankly violated here with two such rights, the right to have the advice of lawyer at every stage of a Court proceeding and the right not to be forced to testify against oneself during such a proceeding. Now, may it please this Court, I'd like to read just a bit further from Judge Desmond's opinion. Now, we accept as evidence a confession extracted during the very course of a judicial proceeding after indictment and surrender on a bench warrant. When the time for investigation has passed and when the prisoner no longer is a suspect, but now a defendant held on the Court process awaiting trial has retained counsel who has warned him not to give evidence to the public authorities. A citizen learning that he has been indicted and that a bench warrant is outstanding against him consults an attorney. The attorney confirming strictly to his professional duty instructs his client forthwith to surrender, arranges for surrender to the representatives of the District Attorney's Office. The lawyer accompanies his client to the place of surrender, warns him not to make any statement then leaves his client in the custody of the officers justifiably confident that the client's rights are safely under the protection of the Court and all of its mandate. The prosecutor in his brief on this appeal reminds us that Spano's attorney could have waited until 10 o'clock the next morning and surrendered his client to the County Court. Of course, he could but as we shall show defendant's rights and immunity would be no different since whether at 7 p.m. or 10 a.m. the surrender was to the Court in obedience to the process.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- whether you can think the case would have been different that the same questioning, for the same duration under or the statement you raised on certain circumstances at the same time by the same personnel had been carried on and a confession then secured that Spano have had no lawyer?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: I don't -- if he had no lawyer?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose that?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: I think the situation would have been the same if he demanded a lawyer, if he asked for a lawyer so long as there was a judicial proceeding in there.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He was advised that the didn't have a lawyer or that he was that the confession was involuntary which is --

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: The confession was involuntary because he was deprived of his right to counsel during a judicial proceeding. We submit most respectfully that there was a judicial proceeding. This was a -- at a pretrial stage. He was entitled to a counsel at every stage of the proceedings.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that the questioning itself do you think was not -- did not deprive the confession from being voluntary?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Not in that sense if we're talking about physical coercion.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This Court hasn't restricted coercion, or physical coercion.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: But this Court --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This Court hasn't held that you require a beating.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Not of those elements separately but this Court has held I believe in the Crooker case that although in that case, the Court held that it was not a violation of due process to deprive a defendant of counsel but that was at the time of arrest and a -- that was at the time of arrest before there was an indictment. And this Court held I believe that to say that the deprivation of counsel in such a stage would be a violation of due process would hamstring the authorities. But in this case, there is no such question and we draw that distinction if we may. We say they have investigated the petitioners' no more a suspect, he is now a defendant. There is a formal accusation against him. It's pending in the Court. He surrenders pursuant to that bench warrant. Therefore, at that time, at the moment of the filing of the indictment, two things happen. Number one, he has an obligation to defend and number two he has the right to attack and at that moment, he certainly could come into Court. He is in Court. There is a judicial proceeding and he could make any kind of a motion that he wanted at that moment. He is certainly -- is entitled to counsel.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: The indictment on which he was tried, was that indictment that had been found prior to this episode?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: That is right, sir.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: No superseding indictment?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: No superseding indictment.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose the -- the judge had been -- and instead of reporting to the District Attorney -- the District Attorney has brought in the judge and it's closed the door, made the same inquiry to the District Attorney in the office, would that change the situation?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: It Wouldn't change. It would be a violation I would say of due process. I don't think if either the judge or the District Attorney or anybody else has the right to question the defendant after the indictment.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose the judge has done before indictment?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well (Voice Overlap) it might raise some other questions before indictment. It might raise some other questions. But if it was a formal charge laid against the defendant, I still believe that he was entitled to counsel on his demand for a counsel.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He'd already been indicted. But suppose the judge after this case had been set on to trial, and he just shuts the door, let the District Attorney ask the big question will that violate the due process?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: I would say, sir, definitely.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: In order to get rid of the case?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: I would say, sir. I would say that in this case if the attorney or the petitioner had been sitting in the courtroom with the petitioner at five minutes to ten waiting for the petitioner to be arraigned and before the clerk of the Court had an opportunity to call the petitioner for arraignment, the District Attorney or the police had snatched the petitioner out of the courtroom, and taken him into a side room outside of the hearing and presence of the defendant and question him, that certainly would have been a violation of due process and the District Attorney's contention that he had a right to question because it was before arraignment, we respectfully submit has no merit because they certainly cannot delay arraignments and say that because the physical act of arraignment didn't take place, they have a right to question. We submit most respectfully to this Court that from the moment of that indictment that defendant was in Court when he surrendered pursuant to the bench warrant he was in Court. He had a right to counsel and that his persistent demands throughout the seven or eight hours of interrogation that he wanted a counsel was a deprivation of due process. Now, we most respectfully urge this Court to reverse the conviction and sentence of death. Thank you.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Mr. Siegal, just so I understand you.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: There was submitted to the jury the issue of whether or not this impression was towards was it not?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Merely under our law of voluntariness in the sense of physical coercion, not on the question of the absence of counsel and not on -- after indictment and not on the question of a failure to advise the petitioner of his rights not to answer after indictment, merely on the question of whether it was coercion due to physical beatings.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Physical brutality enforced right and that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That couldn't be the law of New York any longer (Inaudible) the decisions of this Court. This Court has not so restricted the requirement of the Due Process Clause. I do not believe that your Court would say no matter what the Supreme Court of United States had said, “We restrict voluntariness to physical beatings.”

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Well, they've gone as far as psychological beating.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that's from varying opinions.

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And that was included in the -- in the -- suggest to the jury, then?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, sir, Yes, sir. But the element of the absence of counsel was not included. Thank you.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Anolik.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, Justices of the Court. At the outset, the people respectfully call this Court's attention to page 2 of the transcript of the record furnished by this Court where the New York Court of Appeals said very clearly, the second full paragraph. It is clear in the record and the fact that not now seriously challenge that this confession was neither physically nor psychologically coerced. I think that that is the premise that must be accepted here and I do not believe that petitioner seriously contradicts that fact.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or we wouldn't be concluded by that.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh, I -- I understand that Justice Harlan. I merely pointed that out because of the last remark just to -- there would be no confusion on the record on that point.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What do you understand they meant by psychologically coerced?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say that in view of their citation of a number of decisions by this Court that they had in mind situations such as might have been presented in detention of a defend on duly such as Mallory against United States, things that McNabb against the United States and situations where although no physical beatings were promulgated against the person of defendant that psychologically such pressures that the totality of conduct might have been such that it would have been coerced despite the fact that no physical violence were -- were exerted against him. I think that's probably what they had in mind.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, did they cite any of -- of the cases on which they've relied that those witnesses of persons had subjected to long, continuous, consistent questioning although there was no coercion of any -- no physical action of any kind. Are those the cases they've allowed them?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, the opinion of course they -- they cite Mallory against United States.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That was not one of those (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, that's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's related to this unlawful detention --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- only.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct. The -- the cases that they relied upon were Lisenba against California, Stroble against California, Stein against New York, and cases of that sort. I submit that a perusal of the record, Justice Black, will indicate that there was no psychological coercion even in the sense that this Court has used that term.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I don't know they used the word psychological in connection with the cases by -- and were subjected to long, continued questioning over a long period of hours out of which emerged the confession?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I'm thinking of situations --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That -- that one way it could be stated but it's not a little difficulty (Inaudible) psychological?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I don't recall that this Court has used the term psychological. I was merely trying to answer your question, Justice Black, as best as I could --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- in view of the circumstances of the case. May I continue?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, it -- I don't quite understand what that word means. In fact they are not now seriously challenged (Inaudible) opinion because Judge Desmond says that the same defendant, the same criminal case held under (Inaudible) same court can be subjected to secret midnight questioning out of the reach of any lawyer until he confesses. Now, to be -- believe the (Inaudible) is held for questioning until he confesses. Why shouldn't in the ordinary mode of speaking called that a voluntary confession --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) held subsequent questioning until he confesses that means that not his will but somebody else's will was operating.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Frankfurter, I respectfully submit that. That is not, that Justice -- Judge Desmond's words do not reflect accurately what actually occurred.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm merely suggesting that the vision of opinion was in the Court.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I -- I recognize that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: For one cannot say it's completed.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I was merely quoting the language that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- Justice (Inaudible)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, you -- you were quite within the -- the accurate quotation what Judge Fuld said. My (Inaudible) is not with you -- over you, but over Judge (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, sir. Well, Justice Frankfurter, Justices of the Court, I'd like to point out that at the -- at the -- at a period before petitioner had surrendered, that is about two or three days after the murder had been perpetrated. He telephoned one, Anthony Napolitano who was one of the owners of the Roosevelt bar and tavern outside of which this fight between the deceased and him had occurred and he indicated to him that I'm going to give himself up. This was on the telephone call. I'm going to get a lawyer. Then on February 3rd after the indictment which took place February 1st incidentally, but on February 3rd which was a day before he surrendered, he telephoned a childhood friend, a rookie patrolman named, Patrolman Bruno to which my adversary has a verdict and said to him on the telephone, in effect, that I shot at the deceased. He admitted the shooting of Perlemo and he did not give as much detail but there was no question from the conversation that there was a completed mission that he had shot Perlemo and as a matter of fact in the Court of Appeals of the State of New York at page 60 of the brief there, the -- the defendant in that Court, petitioner here admits that there's no question that he shot Perlemo and that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Anolik.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, justice?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The people who aren't content to rest on that confession to his childhood friend.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He thought it was necessary to introduce this other confession (Voice Overlap). That confession in the context of the circumstances that gave rise to it.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I recognize that, Justice Frankfurter. And if I may, when he was surrendered at about 07:15 or 7:30 p.m. on the 4th, there was no judicial proceeding as such upon the indictment at that point. This questioning was not a judicial proceeding, it was the questioning that might take place when any accused, because we must recognize that an indictment is nothing more than an accusation.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: What about -- what about the bench warrant?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The bench warrant --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: This -- what was done certainly was not an obedience to the -- a command to that word as I understood your adversary reading that law.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: A perusal of that warrant, Justice Brennan, indicates that it said that if the court had adjourned for the term, it was to be -- the petitioner was to be delivered to the warden of city prison.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, had that adjourned?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, it had merely recessed for the day. That court was still in session. It had not adjourned for the term and consequently it was well defended. The provisions of that bench warrant not to deliver him to a warden of the city prison. Now, this defendant was not questioned insistently as a --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: What is there in the bench warrant? I don't have it before me. What is there in the bench warrant that you are suggesting that that authorizes this kind of interrogation?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, It didn't necessarily authorize or forbid this sort of interrogation.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, what -- what was the -- what was its command that the court -- the in term?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, a synopsis of the bench warrant appears on page 2 of the respondent's brief, New York Code of Crim. Proc. Section 301. You are therefore commanded forthwith to arrest the above name and bring him before that court to answer the indictment or if the court have adjourned for the term that --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, if that fail, well, it hasn't adjourned for the terms -- so far.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: It did not --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) to that court to answer the indictment? That's right.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: It had not adjourned for the term.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, now, what -- does that rather imply or does it -- certainly no authority to question in there?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I wouldn't say so, Justice Brennan. This is a situation where this man is an accused in the criminal action, this defendant --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: You mean this could be read and bring in before the Court to answer the indictment unless you want to question and take --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No Justice Brennan, I think it means to bring him at the earliest feasible opportunity which was done here. There was no delay in arraignment in this case, Justice Brennan. He was arraigned at the earliest opportunity.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I don't think so. You got the confession in the middle of the night, or was it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That -- that's not unusual though, Justice Brennan, for a -- a confession to be obtained in the middle of the night. The confession itself was voluntary.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Even though he was (Inaudible) already a defendant in any -- the technical sense.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: In a technical sense he wasn't accused, he had been formally indicted, that is true, Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: A defendant, not an accused. (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: A defendant is an accused, an indictment is an accusation on the laws of the State of New York, as such he wasn't accused, he was a defendant, that's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't -- I haven't read the (Inaudible) case but what Judge Desmond says about it, there was a distinction of it (Inaudible) by the Court of Appeals between a person who became a material witness who at the time he was questioned was not yet an accused or a defendant, was merely a witness. And in this case, where the choice was made, that this wasn't an inquiry by the police to ascertain the guilt of the party, the District Attorney had already committed himself in making the accusation -- I mean made the accusations against this person. Although you're quite right in saying this wasn't the traditional inquiry. There's another way of saying the District Attorney is in the court but he was already in the status of that of a defendant (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, Justice --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Isn't that right?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Frankfurter, for example if this man had not been surrendered or had been apprehended, let's say in other State even if he was under indictment, will this Court mean that that fact would preclude the police officer -- officers would be dispatch to bring him back safe in California. They could not talk to him even if they --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All depends what you mean by talking to him?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Interrogate him.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Or then what do you mean by interrogating? What's the circumstances were under which there was initiated a confession reserved to convict the man?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I agree -- I would agree with you Justice Frankfurter that if the confession --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) in the outset, you take concern on the circumstances under which as in this case, one already under indictment, it detained by the prosecutor. You have series of questions extending over a given time with the circumstances of this case, is that so clearly within or without the decision of this Court if a state can be, admissions, confessions by the defendant not voluntarily use.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, this is before the arraignment upon the indictment Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It was indicted to the -- that is -- none of things are (Inaudible) question, what was the -- what was the process by which this confession was obtained?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: But I don't --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That Judge Desmond is right, that Judge Desmond is right that the -- that the record sustained his conclusion that he was detained and questioned until he confess and I'd like you to repeat that it was not gismo but another person will determine to (Inaudible) of inquiry.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: But Justice Frankfurter I don't think the record will support that. The record here indicates that he was --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's a different story.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes. Well, the record here indicates he was brought up to the District Attorney's Office. There was a casual questioning at about 11 o'clock sandwich was -- were brought in, he was well-fed, in fact he ate more than anyone else. There was --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is not inconsistent with --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No I understand that Mr. Justice Frankfurter. He was then -- after a hiatus of some 45 minutes or -- or an hour brought to the 46th Precinct in the Bronx and there this childhood friend of him, of his to whom he had already given an admission as to the shooting was brought in and spoke to him. And the statement given to the District Attorney following that conversation was substantially consistent with the statement he had given to Patrolman Bruno. Spano did not confess anything to the police by virtue of their questioning, he adhered to the advice of his attorney. The record I believe substantiates the fact of Spano did not take the stance so there's no contest in this record but that it was voluntary in that sense.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But we're not -- we're not here called upon to consider whether what he told his childhood friend was a voluntary explosion of expression (Inaudible) I don't see how that's material even though they were (Inaudible) is the formal, repeated taking down stenographically authenticated testimony or statements that he made if that repetition if you will, for purposes of (Inaudible) more assured conviction of him. If that second statement was under conditions that debar the conclusion as a matter of law this is voluntary, it doesn't help me to have a prior statement that was voluntary.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I agree what you there, Justice Frankfurter. If -- if I understand you correctly that the circumstances under which the confession are taken -- is taken -- is controlling then I would concede that that would be a proper criteria but that is not what the petitioner urges here, the petitioner here urges --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is frequent to countless arguments.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh I understand that I'm merely trying to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) what is controlling.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I understand that Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In fact is if you're not controlled by it.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I understand that but his position here is -- and the people feel that it is an untenable position in view of this Court's prior decisions in this Cicenia against Lagay and Crooker against California cases. The petitioner urges --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There's no indictment neither those --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That -- that is correct Justice Harlan but the very fact of the indictment with nothing more is what petitioner here relies upon as being completely controlling.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He relies in the bench warrant too, does he?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The bench warrant is -- he relies upon both. Now, the record indicates that he did not know an indictment was even outstanding. He was well-aware that he was sought by the police before the indictment was even handed down against them. Under New York law it is a misdemeanor to disclose the fact of indictment. So it is quite probable that he knew nothing of the indictment of such, he merely knew he was wanted and that's the reason he surrendered. I think a perusal of record will indicate that.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What difference would that make?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I don't think it would make too much difference at all Justice Black. I merely pointed it out and answer to Justice Brennan's question.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He was whatever he knew or thought. He was a defendant.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: He was a defendant.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And he was due to be put in jail.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: He was due to be put in jail after being arraigned.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) to the District Attorney's Office.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct. He was surrendered in front of the Distinct Attorney's Office.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Their -- was -- was he surrendered for questioning?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would submit that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That the bench warrant, it didn't say that.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No it didn't but by the same token it didn't forbid it.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, ordinarily yes.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: For example, Justice Black --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible) taken to jail not to the District Attorney to get evidence.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Black, in Avery against Alabama, the defendant there was apprehended in Pittsburgh. He had been indicted in 1934, he was apprehended in 1938. There had been eyewitnesses to the shooting. The record at folio 18 of Avery against Alabama indicates a statement or admissions were taken from him. He was under indictment and this Court held provided, he had counsel from arraignment until final judgment is right for the counsel were utterly absurd.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Was the question raise or brought in --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, the question --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) the other questions raised in that case?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The question was not specifically raised, but --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It didn't even come under it.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct, but I -- I merely point that out that it is not at all unusual for a man who is under indictment, he's merely an accused in a criminal proceeding to say that the time for investigation ceases with an indictment I think is --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible) I -- I don't know but I've never been (Inaudible) where I came from. Is it customary that people on the indictment to take them first to the Office of the District Attorney to let him question him --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- in New York?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, Justice Black, it is not too customary to surrender a man in front of the District Attorney's Office either --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- which is what happened here.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- even if he surrendered in front of the District Attorney's Office. I'm just wandering, is that the custom?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say that where a man is suspected of being a fugitive as Spano was, was he --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, he's -- he's not a suspect, he's indicted.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's -- well, he's a suspect.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But he's indicted --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct Justice Black.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He's the defendant.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He surrendered himself to be arrested.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Is it not -- is it customary in New York to take the people who are indicted that way and to take them to jail, take them to the District Attorney's Office for questioning?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would -- I -- I couldn't --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) I don't know.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Black I couldn't answer that category because I'm not that familiar with what is accordant to other cases. I do know that in situations where indictments have been found, people have been questioned before arraignment.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, has it been taken -- is it the custom to take them to the District --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, they usually would be questioned in the squad room. This was merely for the sake of convenience he was surrendered in front of the District Attorney's Office which happens to be in the Bronx Country Building and was taken up there. He was rented two New York City Police Officers, the District Attorney was present. This was a prearranged surrender. It was at 07:10 p.m. He was taken up there. He was questioned casually. He was federal, well fed, then about a quarter to 12, 12 o'clock he was taken to the 46th squad room which had the 46th squad having jurisdiction of the area wherein the crime was committed and he was questioned there again --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Who -- who questioned him there?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Detectives that questioned him there. The District Attorney was also present. There's no indication of an incessant questioning. Then Patrolman Bruno was called in.Patrolman Bruno spoke to Spano. Spano then gave what the record will reveal to be a clearly voluntary confession, substantially consistent to that to which he had already given prior to the surrender to Patrolman Bruno. Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I just don't see you leap by the hurdle do you not when you say that the man has been under question for all these hours, at the District Attorney's Office, begging for a lawyer. So you leap by the hurdle do you not and say that has to be voluntary.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Black if I may, the record indicates that to only two people according to the record that he'd say, “I want my lawyer,” to Detective Ciccone in the 46th Squad and to Patrolman Bruno, to everyone else according to their own testimony, this is uncontradicted in the record. He said, “See my lawyer.” He did not say “I --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He made that request to a lawyer (Inaudible) The lawyer was there when he said “I need my lawyer,” wasn't he?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, he was not Justice Black.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: The lawyer was never there, he didn't know --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The lawyer surrendered him to the District Attorney.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I'm talking about the District Attorney's lawyer.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh, no -- the District Attorney did not appear in the record that he said to the District Attorney “I want my lawyer”.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, did he say it while the District Attorney was there?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That does not appear in the record. Only Detective Ciccone and Patrolman Bruno indicates that he said to them he wanted his lawyer. There is no evidence in this record, to the best of my recollection I think my recollection is accurate. That he said to the District Attorney, “I want my Lawyer.” There's no evidence of that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But isn't -- I thought your emissary told us that the District Attorney or Assistant that (Inaudible) throughout the interrogation while others came in and out of the room.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That does not appear in the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does -- does it appear from the record? Mr. Siegal said that the -- the interrogation whatever you should make it lasted between (Inaudible) to about (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, there was --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That you challenge that?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, there was a hiatus there of about 45 minutes to an hour because of the fact that they were fed and sandwiches --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No that was about 11 o'clock p.m. Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does the record give the time?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Definitely, there are testimonies for that and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you saying that if we want to reach the record for (Inaudible) of the -- what took place that justified with the trial that an interrogation in the District Attorney's Office one at a time that it isn't from 07:30 to 12:30 --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- from the time that it wasn't persistent. One would find that on the whole if you said a little while ago that it was casual.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I think that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that the preoccupation was in a total to you to getting him to just make a confession.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would not say that. I would -- the last statement I could not agree with. I think that they were certainly interested in getting a statement. What --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did he just -- what -- what would it be? Did they discuss that (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh no, no.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What did they talk about?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: For example they asked him about a red mark on his head. He indicated to them that because of his baldness he suffered from a -- a disease. Later at the trial it indicated from his own doctor that he just -- that he suffered from --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: According to your reading of the record, how many hours is that transaction in the District Attorney's Office to take place?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say from about 7:30 to 11 and perhaps --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That can't be four hours, three and a half hours.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: And then -- then there was a meal and thereafter -- it is difficult to ascertain whether he was requesting or whether he was just transported to the 46th Squad.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But the testimony at the trial in regard to what took place during those hours between 7:30 until 3 whatever the intermission (Inaudible) that the preoccupation of the questioning related to the transaction between other than this unfortunate murder.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say that certainly it would be improper of me to say that they were not anxious to get a confession in the sense that they wanted to verify the statement that they had already received from Patrolman Bruno.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you're saying if we had nothing but that that this would not fall in within -- that this would not satisfy, or this would not be condemnable under the Fourteenth Amendment in the light of our case here, correct?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is correct, Justice Frankfurter, that's my position.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is your position?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, that reversed on what the record may --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That -- that is absolutely --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Absolutely correct, Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what do you say so that then from your point of view, there are really two questions in this case. One, judged by the criteria of our decisions, for this -- on this side or on the other side for inadmissible confession under the Due Process Clause.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Number two, if this was a capital case then he is entitled to have a lawyer. That means at some stage and for some purpose number two, order the trial with that protection of the Due Process Clause the protection of a lawyer.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is correct. Now, I would say that if I can just hope this one particular point that I think this case raises that was not raised in the Crooker or the Cicenia case -- cases that here we have an indictment. Does the fact of an indictment ipso facto alter the situation so that federal rights are violated by questioning? Now, we have seen cases, Lisenba against California where a man is taken out of jail and questioned by an invalid court order and yet the -- the conviction there was upheld. We have seen cases about that. Now, does the fact of an indictment, in fact, I can't answer this question. Thus to the best of my knowledge, it's one of first impression on this Court. Does an indictment ipso facto endow a man with rights superior to those to which any other accused would be entitled?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But Judge Desmond's opinion indicates one consideration on restraints namely the difference between this case and the (Inaudible) case in your court in the Court of Appeals, namely, where a man is detained as a witness, he does not know and has no reason to know or believe that his state is that of an accused with all the difference that that make in the witness of an accused.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, that that may be a fact which in the totality of the situation makes a difference.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He asked that question merely because if a man goes in and talks to him about, he happened to be a very literate or literate person, we discuss (Inaudible) problem and that's the reason of indictment, wouldn't not make or that make -- that make an involuntary confession?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The Perez (ph) case, Justice Frankfurter, the -- the material witness there was in the custody of the warden of the city prison or with the civil --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He was a witness --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- prison rather.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He was a witness, clearly.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's is correct now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He was not an accused, he did not -- he did not stand in the position of a man over whom the (Inaudible) sought of a conviction for murder --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- was upon him.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is correct. Now, of course, in the Perez (ph) case, we do not have a situation where the man was legally and properly in the custody of the police authorities.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But when you say that, when -- when I ask you, when a lawyer surrenders a client, charged with murder I suppose that that means nothing more than murder is not a bailable offense I assume in New York. Is it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, it's not.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Very well, he surrendered him because his -- his client was not -- he can't get bail and there he simply says he has surrendered his body completely should be put in jail. But what consequences follow the surrender? What indications are there beyond that?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But is he not bailable and he doesn't want to escape, he doesn't want to be a fugitive and he assessed it that way, surrendered him. That's all it means --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- assumedly. May I involve that?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, of course --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It doesn't mean go ahead and pressure him all you please doesn't it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No it doesn't necessarily. Now, he had to be booked. He had to be finger printed and photographed. That is true whether the man is under indictment or not. He must be arrested on the indictment on the New York Law. Now, what if this had occurred during that course? He was detained here for the purpose of booking primarily. Now it's true in the District Attorney's Office since he was surrendered that was convenient to take him there. I might add this, Justice Frankfurter, that there is a detective squad of the New York City Police Department that has headquarters in the District Attorney's Office. It's true the questioning here happened to take place in the Office of Assistant District Attorney, Goldsmith. But two New York City detectives to whom he was surrendered were present. They were then brought to the 46th Squad. Now the -- a petitioner here urges that if there had been no indictment and I believe I translated his intentions and his sentiments correctly. He would have no quarrel or whatsoever with this situation. In other words, if 50 people saw Spano shoot Palermo he obviously would be the prime suspect. Yet, if he had not been indicted petitioner would say that perfectly all right to question them all you want, but if you indict him then you can't do anything. Would it be morally correct for the District Attorney here Justice Frankfurter to have withheld an indictment merely to get a statement and then get the indictment when he had all the evidence he needed for it?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That I meant for a clause by that kind of analogy for our petitioner.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I -- I'm merely urging it, Justice Frankfurter. I hope that you don't say --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Do we have the record -- trial record in this Court?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh of course we do.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: The stenographic form?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, in -- in printed form. The people supplied me --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Oh, I see.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- with some copies and the petitioner.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That is in the Attorney's Office?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, I believe there are 10 copies.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Enough for all of us?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I think 10 copies were filed with the Court, if the Court needs any additional we have --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: There is --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: But I believe 10 copies were filed.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I take it from what you say there is no dispute between you that at the time this man was surrendered, the District Attorney's Office knew that he had a lawyer.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No question about it.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: No question about it that and there is no question that they knew he's been indicted.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No doubt about that, too.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And that he was being surrendered pursuant to a bench warrant issued on the indictment. There's no dispute that.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, there was -- there'd be some question as to whether he knew there was a bench warrant outstanding. He may have known, he may not have known.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, he knew he was being surrendered by his lawyer pursuant to an indictment the first time (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, he did not. There's no evidence --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- of that whatsoever. In fact in New York it is a misdemeanor to disclose the fact of indictment prior to a man's --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: What did the District Attorney think he was being surrendered for?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, Spano himself contact this -- now that this -- I -- I must go -- the record to answer that if -- if --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But doesn't the record show it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The record does not specifically show whether he was surrendered pursuant to a bench warrant or not. There is no testimony on behalf of the -- the defendant at all on that --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But certainly --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- point.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- the District Attorney knew that there was an outstanding --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is correct, that thought --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That -- that's conceded isn't it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's conceded.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That there was an outstanding indictment --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That is --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- and the bench warrant was issued on that effect.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Completely conceded.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That's what I was asking you.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, I thought you meant that the defendant --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: No, I was asking whether District Attorney --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh no question about that, I'm sorry if I gave you the -- I didn't mean to mislead you at all.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I may have just spoken to myself.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Right. Oh no -- no doubt about that at all, Justice Brennan.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: When was the confession and this confession to prove?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The day before his surrender of February 3rd at about 9:20 p.m. He telephoned Bruno whom he had known for a number of years. Told him what he had done.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And who is Bruno?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Bruno is a rookie patrolman, a childhood friend of Spano's. At that time he was a rookie patrolman in New York City Police Force. Bruno testified that he admitted the killing “What should I do?” he asked him. Bruno suggested that he give himself up. He then indicated to Bruno that he was going to get himself a lawyer and he was going to surrender. Bruno, pursuant to police regulations I would assume testified. He immediately called his superiors in the 46th Squad and advised them of the fact of this call and apparently they gave him some further instructions. That does not appear exactly what the further instructions were.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. -- Mr. Anolik, in your brief --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- we have just scanned isn't it that the uncontradicted evidence clearly revealed that petitioner's confession was completely voluntary. That is your position --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That would be my position.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in here. But you don't -- you don't -- as it was authenticate that. You simply almost take it for granted and you rely talking on what Justice Brennan said. But clearly it seems to me that, that's a vital part of the case -- that's on page --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, I'm -- I'm familiar with that, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If -- if this -- if this confession, lawyer or no lawyer, murder or no murder was voluntary, even legal, constitutional the Fourteenth Amendment said, is that qualified? That that is the only other problem is the denial of access to his lawyer (Inaudible) but you sort of take that for granted?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I don't think the petitioner even seriously contradicts that.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That issue was submitted to the jury, was it not?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: It was fully submitted and as a matter of fact --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: The petitioner doesn't question the instructions under which he was in submitted to the jury, does it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Not the instructions but he --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And that issue was -- were resolved by the jury wasn't it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But that is not binding on us.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As that document points out.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Frank --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It had case after case in which the court and jury left and if the court left an issue of voluntariness to the jury and the jury resolved it against people, State in favor the defendant and this Court has said as a matter of law.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's not an issue that determinable by a jury.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I understand it Justice.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And so that's the question in this case.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It may be right on the record but it turns on what the testimony was.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Not merely and -- and as you say he didn't the stand. Cross-examination also matters --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- what the court has brought out.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The ones really would to have read, I don't know how many pages of testimony to say whether it's a matter of law. This was a punishable issue and the jury could so find, if it could so find and it did so find.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But the fact that the jury so find -- found as needed it could have sought for.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I -- I recognize that, Your Honor, I know that this Court certainly have the duty and the right to make an independent review of the record. May I point out Justice Frankfurter that from folios 1833 to 2022 of the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Let me put that down.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Of the record, the folios. There's an elaborate voir dire conducted by Mr. Siegal here on behalf of the -- of the petitioner that's to whether or not the confession should go into evidence and it was not until that or the confession even went into evidence and then there was instructions --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Who -- who were the people who were summoned under voir dire? The policemen or the District Attorney?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I think it was Detective Farrell who was summoned in the voir dire.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How about the Assistant District Attorney?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, he was not called for the stand. He was available there obviously but he was not called for the stand.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He wasn't --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: He -- in fact he tried --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) people to -- to ascertain the circumstances under which this questioning took place.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, he was not called by the people. In the final analysis, Mr. Chief Justice and Justices of the Court, I think that the focal point of this issue is whether an indictment making a man a defendant it is true but being no more than an accusation of guilt means (1) that investigation -- further investigation is precluded and the people contend, it does not mean that because under New York statutory law, less proof is necessary for an indictment then is necessary for a conviction. (2) Does the fact of an indictment, ipso facto preclude questioning of an accused? If the answer --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I don't --- I don't see how even if your adversary has (Inaudible) I didn't so understand. Certainly, Justice Frankfurter said he couldn't buy or does change his analysis. Why do you say that -- settles it all, just makes all the difference in the world if he's indicted?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Black --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible) if there was none but can -- might -- might possibly (Inaudible) if there isn't.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I didn't come to number three yet, Justice Black. Number three would be that if the record itself discloses that not withstanding an indictment and not withstanding the other matters that the confession itself was taken under circumstances, the totality of circumstances that would have rendered it involuntary not withstanding the finding of New York Court, then, of course this Court would -- would have a duty to reverse, too.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I think the nub -- at least for me, the nub of the problem is that one (Inaudible) to Judge Desmond being independent in the case -- criminal case held under the process of the same court can -- it is now decided being subjected to secret midnight questioning out of reach of any lawyer until he confessed it. Now, if that is the -- if the record sustained that characterization by Judge Desmond it is one thing because this judgment is another thing.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I'll point out, Justice Frankfurter, that the defendant here was arraigned promptly. Justice Desmond relies upon or Judge Desmond relies upon cases such as People against McMann and People against 01:00:33 which you will see in this opinion.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Im not -- every time you -- you said that before by which you mean only that he was arraigned at 10 o'clock the following morning.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Brennan.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Are you suggesting that to say had not obtained the confession at 3:30 that morning he would have been arraigned at 10 o'clock?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, of course, I can't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And how you could prove it either one -- what -- what's the significance in this case that he wasn't in fact arraigned at 10:00 since they already had the confession at the time of arraignment?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: By the same token Justice Brennan, they had -- they had a statement from him before he even surrendered.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you didn't rely on that -- I'm (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No. We did not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No. that's correct Justice Frankfurter. But we didn't rely on that exclusively.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Exclusively. It doesn't matter exclusively if -- if -- and this had been -- it wouldn't be the first time that a District Attorney try to over proved his case. That's not unfamiliar to anybody who's been in District Attorney's Office or outside of it. If in fact, although you have all the evidence that was necessary for conviction, who seal of all enterprise are one it was sort to get them all formal and more detailed confession under circumstances characterized by what I have read from Judge Desmond then that confession is not admissible under our decision.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And those terms on to exact facts of circumstances of this case.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Or you can say, Justice Frankfurter that I think a perusal of the record will indicate that Justice Desmond's -- Judge Desmond's statement is not wise. I would add this fact that in the case at bar, it is fairly clear, I would say clear beyond doubt that the questioning of Spano was to cover a hiatus. There were eyewitnesses to a -- beating, how severe that beating was, is a matter of dispute. But there are eyewitnesses to that beating. Then there was an eyewitness to the actual shooting. That hiatus between the beating and the shooting was unaccounted for and that is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean the statement who is this childhood friend?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct. That is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The District Attorney didn't need tell further evidence to fill in (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, Justice Frankfurter. Not -- I'm saying not. I'm merely saying that they were probing and attempting to verify certain practice that'd already been brought to their attention.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes. But --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: They could have found the reverse. Now, I -- I submit that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I must have (Inaudible) the District Attorney must have thought it was relevant to get those items cleared out.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And partly, I suppose it might have been a self-defense -- defense.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Could have been.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could have been that? So, if there were items --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- whether it's cumulatively or independently necessary that the District Attorney thought it was necessary to make a case.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: But there were --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes. Except that they were not sine qua non.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I don't know but -- lawyers have to make judgment --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- as to what is necessary or desirable for the success of the particular side that needs evidence.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Of course, a District Attorney, Justice Frankfurter, is not a magistrate and as such, I -- I think, the Court will concede had not duty.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: At least, it wouldn't make any difference if -- if there haven't been the District Attorney but merely police officers by (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I see. I saw your position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was this man's execution stayed by the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh, yes. His execution was stayed until the final determination by this Court.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You've said several times that Justice Desmond's statement was not correct because to the effect that they kept them there to get a -- until they get a confession?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I don't think it's warranted --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: What --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What possible purpose could they have had in keeping him there and questioning him as they did except to get a confession?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
