turns.text: I think that they were seeking to get a confession but he is assuming that they would have kept him there indefinitely, which I don't think is a fair inference from the record.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Of course, we have no -- no basis on which to prophesy exactly how long they would have kept him.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I think that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But your -- your problem is I think not to decide that show that Justice Desmond was not right in that but try to show that by the second (Inaudible) the way they did, it doesn't conflict with our previous opinion as to due process.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I've been trying to transmit --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: On the evidence but I -- I do not yet see where you have established that Justice Desmond's statement is incorrect at all.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, on the evidence, Justice Black, we have no contradictory evidence whatsoever by the defendant because he did not take the stand. Incidentally --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I know he did it, it's a question of uncontradicted evidence.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Right. The State, that is the respondent here, questioned this defendant from 7:30 to about 11:00. There were sandwiches brought up.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But did they go out to get the sandwiches or they just stayed there while they were questioning him?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Someone went out for sandwiches.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did he get out or did he stay there for questioning during at (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: He -- he stayed there, but the record is not at all clear that he was questioned incessantly. In other words, there is nothing in the record to indicate incessant -- insistent --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, there may not be but what other earthly object that anybody would think about that having in therefore during that time except to question him and keep on questioning?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Except that the -- the testimony of Detective Lehrer and some other detectives would indicate that the discussions did not only center about that crime. There would -- there were all -- other matters discussed.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What other matter?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: They -- they discussed his physical appearance and that they talked -- I -- I can't remember --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But what other subjects did they discuss that didn't have something to do with -- some kind of possible relevance under this crime?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, off hand, I couldn't think of any, Justice Black.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can we agree that he was in the control of the police and the prosecutor, uninterrupted. I'm not now saying what took place but he was in the control of the police and the prosecutors from 7:30 to 3:00 when his confession was taken down.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That -- that would be correct -- that would be definitely correct.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: What does the record show that finally prevailed upon him to confess at 3 o'clock in the morning?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: A -- a strong --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: His name is Bruno?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, a conversation with Bruno.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And that Bruno was instructed to tell him a certain story that clearly submitted in the record (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No. Bruno indicated that he was told to give him whatever story that might try to convince him to -- to make a statement. In other words, if you can play upon his sympathy do that but he said his job was never threatened.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, but up to that time the District Attorney's inquiry had elicited the information to fill in what you called a hiatus, correct?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct. He had said nothing, he adhered to his attorney's advice.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: So, what does the record --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The record then indicates that after a conversation with Bruno. He then --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Conversation about what -- what was the report (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: The purpose of the conversation was the fact that he had embarrassed Bruno by calling him the day before that Bruno might get into difficulty because of the fact that Spano, an accused murderer had called him the day before and that he would -- would appreciate it as a matter of friendship to a confess, to give some statement. And the statement he gave was substantially consistent with the statement that he read, what he'd given to Bruno.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, Bruno -- Bruno I gather in representing that he -- Bruno might be in trouble and that this might have consequences for Bruno's wife and child and so forth. He was not telling the truth was he?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say, that Bruno was -- as I say -- shall we -- putting it on I would say.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: He was not telling the truth.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say not telling the truth in the sense that --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: In the sense --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: What (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: that he was not telling the truth.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I'm sorry. He's not telling the truth.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And Bruno was told -- and Bruno was told to play on his sympathy?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Bruno was told, I believe that the record would -- would indicate that he was told to play on his sympathy or to use any other --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: To use the lifelong friendship.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's what, use his lifelong friendship to -- to see if he could elicit a -- a confession from him.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And you're going to say that anything -- any other thing --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- something you -- you used those words to say --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, if I did then, Chief Justice, I -- I didn't intend to. In other words, I think the record indicates that Bruno was not told to get a confession or else.He was told to go in there and talk to him. See if you can get him to --to say something. It wasn't the situation where, “Well, do what you can, you better get it or -- or otherwise you're going to be fired or anything of that sort.” That was not done.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What page is the matter (Inaudible) How long have been -- how long the District Attorney (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: About 3:00 p.m. or 3:00 a.m. in the morning I would say that Bruno --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Forced the answer (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's right. Although that the record will indicate Justice Harlan that Bruno was in the District Attorney's general offices, although not in the same room with Spano earlier in evening. He was not called in at that point. Now, (Inaudible), he's a member of police force but it was not until around 3:00 a.m. I think in the morning that he was told to speak to Spano and out following that conversation.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Anolik, I now have these three verse of arguments, could you without wasting your time give me a reference to the page where Bruno's testimony is to be found, could you do that for me?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I probably can.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Beginning at page -- folio 2250, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: 2250. Thank you very much. When was this crime committed?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: On January 22nd, 1957.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And when was the trial? How (Inaudible) When was the trial -- it's been known to you. This must have been a quick trial. He was a -- have been in the member (Inaudible) over five months. He was a very -- very much of a rookie, wasn't he?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes. He was quite a rookie. Are there any questions? The people would --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You haven't dealt with the problem which for you seems to be crucial in this case namely, assuming you're right about everything concerning the confession. What about the fact that he asked for a lawyer and didn't get one?Did he ask to see his lawyer or did he -- didn't have access to his lawyer. What about that?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say this but --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's very important. This is a murder case.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's quite true that this is a murder case. This man was not just plucked out of the street, Justice Frankfurter as a possible suspect. He was -- an eyewitness had already identified him. He'd already given a statement to Patrolman Bruno. And he was questioned. The Court -- this Court has often recognized that and has condemned plucking a man as a possible suspect and hammering away and until they get a confession. In this case they had a man who they certainly have probable cause to believe had committed this crime.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I should think a (Inaudible) all the more reason for seeing that he has the protection of a lawyer.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: He had had the advice of an attorney who's -- who turned him over to two police officers in the District Attorney on the street.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, not only should be incarcerated but he couldn't be allowed out on bail. Was there any other purpose for turning him over?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say that -- that would be --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Doesn't he have -- turning him over so that he shouldn't when he asked to see the lawyer he shouldn't be -- should be disallowed?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, there's nothing in the record to indicate, Justice Frankfurter that there was any agreement that he would not be questioned. On the contrary, since he was admonished not to say anything, as the New York Court of Appeals recognizes, apparently interrogation was anticipated otherwise why admonish him to say nothing.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What from your -- your reading of the record, what -- does the record show as to a demand by him to see his lawyer to have him present to consult him?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I say this --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was there a denial of such a request?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Detective Ciccone testified that Spano asked to see his lawyer. He said he then attempted to locate his lawyer's name in the telephone book and was unsuccessful. The attorney --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was the lawyer a known member of your bar?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: He was known but this was the 46th one. He was then -- he then asked Patrolman --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is that?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the significance of that?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, in other words, Detective Ciccone wouldn't necessarily have known this particular lawyer.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, he might not have known (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct, but --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Wouldn't there be a responsibility in -- in having him take steps so that he would find out where that lawyer was?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Frankfurter, all I can say is that the record indicates that Detective Ciccone alleges that he attempted to find the name in the telephone book and could not. So, there's nothing further --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: The insisting could certainly do him because he had received Spano from the --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Brennan.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: The attorney didn't he?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: But that is assuming, Justice Brennan that a direct request was made to the -- the Assistant. But there's no evidence of that at all.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you -- you think that a policeman would ask him -- to whom a -- an indicted person gives the name of his lawyer, who can't find it in a telephone maybe because he didn't get the spelling right, is absurd when taking no further step (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No. I -- I think that the -- the -- I think, the police officer should be reprimanded that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, but it could -- if it's a question of getting protection from legal advice, legal advice that the -- the suffering or rather the hurt, the denial that's involved in his right to get a lawyer reduces a State. Every -- every police have the -- every prosecutor is part of the State for that purpose?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would agree. If the Court, this Court has passed upon similar problems, Justice Frankfurter, not while on indictment was extant, that's true but certainly this Court has passed on in the Cicenia and the Crooker cases.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In the Crooker case, this isn't the Crooker case. This is --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I know it's not.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Very different situation. Seeing the nature -- the individual had experienced -- it was almost that he was a lawyer himself etcetera -- etcetera.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, in the Lisenba case, there was a request for counsel and counsel was not furnished. I believe in Stroble there was a question or there was a request for counsel and counsel was not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But the question of having a counsel whether was if it was taken. The counsel that was -- wasn't asking for a lawyer. Not denying access to lawyer, what you have got?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, of course, in -- I believe in -- in Lisenba he had a lawyer and the -- it was -- they took him out on -- on May 2nd or something and questioned him --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There's a question there on whether it was or wasn't a voluntary confession.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct and I think the same issue would have to be faced here, Justice Frankfurter or that was not --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was this lawyer listed in the telephone booklet?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No, question about it, he was.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: He was?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: It could have been due to a misunderstanding of the pronunciation of the name. I don't know but I -- I'm not trying to justify Detective Ciccone, Chief Justice Warren. Make no mistake about that. I merely say that his testimony stands that he couldn't locate him.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What more advice did the lawyer have given if they called him and brought him up to than that which he had already given them and that which the man kept saying, ”My lawyer has told me not to talk.”

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That all he could (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's about all he could have done. That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, maybe when you say that the lawyer had admonished him because he anticipated questioning maybe it'd be a little clearer, if you'd say because he feared he would be questioned.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's another -- another way of putting it, Justice Black.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, there's one more thing. If the lawyer had been present, the lawyer would have interposed his will.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And imposed his will on his client as against the attempted assertion of will by the District Attorney.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's quite correct. Now, of course, in State v. Bump which is a New Jersey decision cited on my brief, the court there indicates where a -- a somewhat similar but not identical situation obtained that while an attorney should be present to defend the client an attorney is not necessary to protect the client from his own voluntary statements. And if this was a voluntary statement, then the people submit no federal right was produced.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But for how many hours after -- after the District Attorney and the police know that a man has -- has a lawyer and know who he is and -- and the defendant has protested that his lawyer has told him not to talk and he doesn't want to talk. For how many hours can they question him in order to overcome his will in that regard and get the statement that they desire?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I would say that -- of course, it's a difficult question to answer. I would say that that's where the significance of a prompt arraignment might come into issue. If this man had been detained, shall we say until the 6th of February, another full day and then the -- the confession obtained. Then we might say that here they went too far. But in this case, this -- this specific case they arraigned him promptly as soon as the courts reopened that morning.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, could they have continued to -- to interrogate him all night long and then brought him in at 10 o'clock with the same result?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I suppose, it would be conceivable but unlikely in view of the fact they have to have him fingerprinted, booked and photographed before he was brought into that Court and that was done.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. But I mean if they had done -- if they had done it, the situation would be the same as far as you can see as long as they arraigned him on time.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say that, there again, assuming that the record indicates the confession of itself was voluntary, yes. I'd have to say that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Even though he was protesting from -- from that time on until 10 o'clock in the morning that he didn't want to talk, that his -- his counsel had advised him, not to him, that he wanted to see his lawyer and so forth.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I would say that to be consistent. I have to say that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: But may I say this, Mr. Chief Justice Warren, that only two witnesses, Patrolman Bruno and Detective Ciccone ever testified that he asked for his lawyer that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- the other --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How many should he have to do when people are coming in and going out of the room? Should he have to look at every man and tell him --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- specifically --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is it not sufficient for him to -- to say to whoever is in charge of questioning him that his lawyer has advised him not to talk. That he doesn't propose to talk and -- and the client should do it. And then for four or five hours, he -- he declines to make a statement for that reason. Does he have to be meticulous about seeing that he made the same statement to every single one

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- of the people?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Of course, not.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, then what -- why do you keep (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, I -- I --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- they only said it to two of the detectives who where there.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I -- I merely emphasize that from this point of view only, Justice Warren, that there is no direct evidence in this record that that request was made to the District Attorney or to the two detectives to whom he surrendered. To them it's true. They testified, he said, “You'd better see my lawyer about that.” Now, that -- that is what the record says, Justice Warren. I'm not attempting to characterize it one way or the other. We're bound by the record. There's no question about that. Now, if -- if this meant that the questioning here, if it was voluntary, the people submit that there's no reason to invoke the Fourteenth Amendment because an indictment was extant. If it was not voluntary, of course, there is obviously, a Fourteenth Amendment problem.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What the -- now, what is the practice in New York after arraignment where a man was incarcerated waiting -- what instructions do the police have as to whether it's admissible or mandatory or not?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Instructions are not the question at all after that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you say that everything up until arraignment (Inaudible)

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Typically, that is what institutes the judicial proceeding. Section 144 of the Code of Criminal Procedure of New York speaks of judicial proceedings commencing with the filing of an indictment but that section has reference to statutes of limitations. And in only in that sense that a judicial proceeding start and certainly, I think that this Court will recognize that this interrogation was not a judicial proceeding upon that indictment as the dissent --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That's precisely -- that's precisely what the petitioner is complaining about. They said that after indictment what -- the only thing that can follow properly under the law and Constitution is an arraignment and the trial which are judicial proceedings. And he says here that this --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- after an indictment instead they actually have these proceedings in the backroom of the station house.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Justice Stewart, if an indictment, as I say, precludes any questioning whatsoever, then, of course, my cause is -- is lost. I concede that. If this Court is prepared to say that the fact of an indictment in a state court precludes any further investigation, any further question then I -- I concede that my cause is lost. But I respectfully submit that this Court is not -- is not required to make such a determination in view of its decisions and in view of this record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. -- before you sit down. I'd like to ask you now that I have cast my eye over the pages of Bruno's testimony and I want to be sure that I haven't -- in my skimpy reading of it misread. In the first place, there's testimony by him that Mr. (Inaudible) came at 4:00 a.m. not 3:00 a.m. as both of you have said, but much important than that, he made several efforts to get -- what's his name-- Spano as he thought, didn't he?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, he did.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And Spano stood his ground and wouldn't talk.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And Gannon sent him back again and again.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That -- that's correct. He sent them back several times.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That there was a refusal to talk.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's a --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That there was an insistence by (Inaudible) friend Bruno to get him to talk.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: I -- I would say that the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Lieutenant Gannon his superior, this fellow (Inaudible) he was brought down from the academy, wasn't it?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, he happened to live in the neighborhood.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes. But he wasn't part of the police --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Oh, no, no.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And somewhere they found out that he's a great credibility, they got hold of him.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well -- no. He telephoned him --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Just --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- a day before this. So they knew about this conversation, the day before you see.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But he -- he stood his ground at the insistence of his boyhood friend, he wouldn't talk, several times that he says -- and at that time he told Lieutenant Gannon that they could get no statement out of this man, is that right? I did and you went back into the room? Yes. At whose direction? The Lieutenant and come -- what it was that he was said (Inaudible) don't worry etcetera. (Inaudible) a long course of -- or an effort or rather serious continuing effort to have Spano speak freely, is that right?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And -- and he did, he was not forthcoming until to these -- these hours in which took place what you've said took place.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And finally at 4:00 in the morning if I have to understand you.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Well, the -- although the statement of the stenographer indicate an earlier time --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: -- there's some --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Anyhow --

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- one wouldn't (Inaudible) about an hour and a half.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: No. I -- I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is the course of events by which eventually this statement came to pass.

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what you have just stated?

turns.name: IRVING ANOLIK
turns.text: Yes. That's correct. If there are no further questions Chief Justice, Justices of the Court. Thank you very much for your indulgence.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Siegal. Are you finished?

turns.name: HERBERT S. SIEGAL
turns.text: Yes, thank you.
